title: One-Step Transformation of Yeast
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"4p7lf","text":"Quick and easy method to transform a free plasmid into either budding or fission yeast.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Add 90 uL of 50% Polyethylene Glycol 3350 to a 1.5 mL microcentrifuge tube. undetermined Polyethylene Glycol 3350 Contributed by users

Add 10 uL of 0.2 N Lithium Acetate to the microcentrifuge tube. undetermined In this case, 0.2 N Lithium Acetate and 0.1 M Lithium Acetate are the same thing. Lithium acetate dihydrate Contributed by users
Pellet yeast from 1 mL liquid culture or take a toothpick full of freshly grown yeast on a plate and resuspend in the solution.
Add 1 ug of DNA and briefly vortex. undetermined
Incubate at 45°C for 30 minutes to 1 hour, vortexing briefly every 10 minutes. 01:00:00 I have found that usually 30 minutes is just fine. Some people swear that longer is better though.
Add 1 mL water to the tube and pipette to mix, then centrifuge at 14,000 RPM for 15 seconds. undetermined 00:00:15 Nuclease-free Water Contributed by users

Remove the supernatant and resuspend the pellet in 200 uL water, then spread on selective plates and incubate at 30°C for 3-6 days. undetermined Budding yeast should show colonies in 3-4 days while fission yeast may take 4-6. This difference has to do with the type of medium classified as minimal medium for each organism, along with fission yeast having a longer generation time. Nuclease-free Water Contributed by users
creator: Alan Cone
